answers.value:
I am requesting that my son, Ethan J. Carlson, be excused from participation in all statewide standardized assessments for the 2025–2026 school year, including the MCA/MTAS Reading, Mathematics, and Science assessments, as well as ACCESS or Alternate ACCESS for ELLs (if applicable).

My decision is based on several factors that we have carefully considered as a family over the past few years:

1. Impact on Ethan’s anxiety and well‑being: Ethan has a history of test-related anxiety that began in 3rd grade when he took his first round of MCA tests. In the weeks leading up to those exams, we noticed clear changes in his behavior: difficulty sleeping, increased stomachaches on school mornings, and a lot of self‑critical talk (for example, he would say things like “If I don’t do well, my teacher will be disappointed in me,” or “What if I’m the only one who fails?”). During that testing window we received two calls from the school nurse because he complained of headaches and nausea during the test sessions. After that experience we worked with his pediatrician and school counselor, who both agreed that high‑stakes testing appears to be a trigger for his anxiety.

2. Preference for multiple measures of learning: We strongly believe that Ethan’s progress is better reflected through his day‑to‑day classroom work, ongoing teacher assessments, projects, and the regular unit tests and quizzes he already completes. His teachers already provide detailed feedback on his reading, writing, math, and science skills through progress reports, parent‑teacher conferences, and the school’s online gradebook. We see more value in these multiple, low‑stakes measures than in a single, high‑pressure exam that takes place once a year and does not provide timely or detailed feedback that can help him improve.

3. Instructional time and stress during the testing window: From our experience and from information we’ve received from the school, we understand that the testing window often requires significant blocks of time for practice tests, test‑taking strategy lessons, and the assessments themselves. In previous years Ethan has told us that several days in a row were largely dedicated to testing or test preparation, with less time for regular instruction, art, music, and hands‑on science activities that he genuinely enjoys and that keep him engaged in school. We would prefer that, during those weeks, he continue with regular classroom learning rather than participating in additional standardized testing.

4. Alignment with family values about learning: As a family, we emphasize growth, curiosity, and the development of strong study habits rather than performance on standardized tests. We do not want Ethan to equate his self‑worth or academic potential with a single test score. We have had many conversations with him about effort, resilience, and learning from mistakes, and we feel that stepping back from high‑stakes testing at this point in his development is more consistent with those values.

5. Prior review of district and state information: We have read the Minnesota Department of Education’s information about statewide assessments, including how the results are used for accountability, school improvement, and federal reporting. We understand that by opting him out, Ethan may receive a “not proficient” or similar notation in state records and that his non‑participation may affect school and district participation rates. We also understand that the district may still administer its own local assessments and classroom tests, and we are not requesting that he be excused from those routine evaluations.

We want to emphasize that this decision is not a reflection of any concern with Ethan’s teachers or school. We have been very pleased with the quality of instruction he receives at Highland Park Elementary and are grateful for the support his teachers have provided. Our refusal applies only to the statewide standardized testing program for this school year.

We respectfully request that the school make alternative arrangements for Ethan during any statewide testing sessions (for example, allowing him to read independently, work on regular class assignments, or complete enrichment activities in a supervised setting). We are happy to work with his classroom teacher and the school office to ensure that his schedule during the testing window is clear and that he is not inadvertently placed in a testing environment.

If there are any additional district forms, acknowledgments, or follow‑up steps required to finalize this opt‑out for the 2025–2026 school year, please let us know. We can be reached by phone or email and are more than willing to discuss this decision with school staff or administration if needed.
